items.q: Which letter is typically SILENT at the end of French words like 'petit' or 'froid'?
items.why: Students try to pronounce every letter. Final consonants in French are usually silent (CaReFuL exceptions: c, r, f, l). Petit is pronounced 'puh-TEE', not 'puh-TEET'.

items.q: Which verbs take 'être' as their auxiliary in the passé composé?
items.correct: Movement/state-change verbs (DR & MRS VAN DER TRAMP) and all reflexive verbs.
items.why: Students forget reflexives. Both groups use être: aller/venir/monter, etc. AND every reflexive verb (je me suis lavé). With être, the past participle agrees with the subject.
Students use 'ends in -e = feminine' as a rule. Many exceptions exist: le problème, le système, le programme, le poème - all masculine despite the -e ending. Gender must often be memorised.
Correct answer: C'est. “Il y a” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Where does colour go in French?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: After noun. “Before noun” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many describing words listed: c'est/il est/elle est?
items.why: The real figure is 3. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
